content.full_text:
Memorandum

To: Opinion Request Participants

Date: June 8, 1978

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
Barbara Campbell

Subject: Reportability of Commissioned Study

The antismoking initiative will appear on the November, 1978, ballot. Assume the following: A non-California cigarette manufacturer is considering commissioning a study by an independent research institute on the effects of smoke inhaled by nonsmokers. It is expected that the study will be completed before the November, 1978, general election. It is not known whether there will be competitive bidding for the study.

The results of the study will determine whether or not the study will be used for political purposes. If the study concludes there are harmful effects to nonsmokers, the study will not be used. However, if the conclusion is there are no harmful effects, the study will be part of the manufacturer's campaign strategy. The manufacturer is currently filing Form 461. When and how much of the study is reportable? (I.e. when the contract is entered into? When the study is completed and determined to be politically helpful? When the results are disseminated?)

In a February 25, 1976, letter to the Federal Energy Administration's Administrator (a copy of which is attached), Mickey Bennett outlined five facts on which the Commission staff relied to conclude FEA's contract with the University of Texas was reportable as an expenditure in connection with FEA's opposition to Proposition 15.

Should those same factors be applied to this situation? Are there additional factors that should be considered?

BC:plh
Attachment

At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff concluded that the cost of the study would be reportable if the study were used for a communication that expressly advocated the defeat of the measure. (See letter to Pessner.)

sections.question_synthetic: A cigarette manufacturer considering commissioning a study on smoke effects, potentially for use in campaign strategy against an antismoking ballot initiative, seeks clarification on when and how much of the study cost must be reported under FPPC regulations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff concluded that study costs are reportable only if the study is actually used in a communication that expressly advocates the defeat of the ballot measure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum-style advice letter dated June 8, 1978, referencing a June 12, 1978 Opinion Request meeting conclusion. Contains both the question and conclusion clearly stated. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but content structure clearly identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The antismoking initiative will appear on the November, 1978, ballot. Assume the following: A non-California cigarette manufacturer is considering commissioning a study by an independent research institute on the effects of smoke inhaled by nonsmokers. It is expected that the study will be completed before the November, 1978, general election. It is not known whether there will be competitive bidding for the study. The results of the study will determine whether or not the study will be used for political purposes. If the study concludes there are harmful effects to nonsmokers, the study will not be used. However, if the conclusion is there are no harmful effects, the study will be part of the manufacturer's campaign strategy. The manufacturer is currently filing Form 461. When and how much of the study is reportable? (I.e. when the contract is entered into? When the study is completed and determined to be politically helpful? When the results are disseminated?) Should those same factors outlined in a February 25, 1976, letter regarding FEA's contract with the University of Texas be applied to this situation? Are there additional factors that should be considered?

CONCLUSION: At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff concluded that the cost of the study would be reportable if the study were used for a communication that expressly advocated the defeat of the measure.

embedding.summary: This memorandum addresses reportability of a commissioned research study on smoke effects by a cigarette manufacturer in connection with an antismoking ballot initiative. The FPPC staff concluded that costs would be reportable only if the study is used in express advocacy communications against the measure.
